examples.title: Algorithm: General Disk Scheduling Framework
examples.description: Pseudocode structure showing how disk scheduling algorithms work, with modular design for FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DiskScheduling(input_queue, head_position, disk_range)
// disk_range = [min_cylinder, max_cylinder]
INPUT: List of cylinder requests, initial head position, disk boundaries
OUTPUT: Order of service and total head movement

BEGIN
 SET current_head = head_position
 SET total_movement = 0
 SET service_order = []
 SET remaining_requests = input_queue.copy()
 
 WHILE remaining_requests is not empty DO
 SELECT next_request USING scheduling_algorithm(remaining_requests, current_head)
 APPEND next_request TO service_order
 SET movement = ABS(next_request - current_head)
 SET total_movement = total_movement + movement
 SET current_head = next_request
 REMOVE next_request FROM remaining_requests
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN service_order, total_movement
END

// ============================================
// Algorithm: FCFS (First Come First Served)
// ============================================
ALGORITHM FCFS_Select(remaining_requests, current_head)
INPUT: Pending requests, current head position
OUTPUT: First request in queue

BEGIN
 RETURN remaining_requests[0] // Simply return the first request
END

// ============================================
// Algorithm: SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)
// ============================================
ALGORITHM SSTF_Select(remaining_requests, current_head)
INPUT: Pending requests, current head position
OUTPUT: Request closest to current head

BEGIN
 SET min_distance = INFINITY
 SET closest_request = NULL
 
 FOR EACH request IN remaining_requests DO
 SET distance = ABS(request - current_head)
 IF distance < min_distance THEN
 SET min_distance = distance
 SET closest_request = request
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 RETURN closest_request
END

// ============================================
// Algorithm: SCAN (Elevator Algorithm)
// ============================================
ALGORITHM SCAN_Select(remaining_requests, current_head, direction, disk_range)
INPUT: Pending requests, current head, direction (LEFT/RIGHT), disk bounds
OUTPUT: Next request in scan direction

BEGIN
 SET candidates = []
 
 FOR EACH request IN remaining_requests DO
 IF direction = RIGHT AND request >= current_head THEN
 ADD request TO candidates
 ELSE IF direction = LEFT AND request <= current_head THEN
 ADD request TO candidates
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 IF candidates is not empty THEN
 IF direction = RIGHT THEN
 RETURN MIN(candidates) // Closest in right direction
 ELSE
 RETURN MAX(candidates) // Closest in left direction
 END IF
 ELSE
 REVERSE direction // No more requests in current direction
 REPEAT SCAN_Select with new direction
 END IF
END

// ============================================
// Algorithm: LOOK (Enhanced SCAN)
// ============================================
ALGORITHM LOOK_Select(remaining_requests, current_head, direction)
INPUT: Pending requests, current head, direction
OUTPUT: Next request (same as SCAN but no disk end travel)

BEGIN
 // Similar to SCAN but does NOT go to disk boundaries
 // Only goes to the last request in each direction
 // Implementation identical to SCAN in structure
 // Difference: no check for disk_range boundaries
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode provides a modular framework for disk scheduling algorithms. FCFS simply processes requests in arrival order. SSTF uses a greedy approach to select the closest request. SCAN acts like an elevator, servicing all requests in one direction before reversing. The key difference in LOOK is that it only travels to the last pending request rather than to the disk edge.

examples.title: Python: Implementation of All Disk Scheduling Algorithms
examples.description: Complete Python implementation with FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, and C-LOOK algorithms with visualizations and performance comparison
examples.code:
"""
Disk Scheduling Algorithms Implementation
============================================
Topic: Operating Systems - Disk Scheduling
Example data: Queue = [98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67], Head = 53
"""

import math
from typing import List, Tuple, Optional

def calculate_head_movement(service_order: List[int], start: int) -> int:
 """Calculate total head movement from service order."""
 total = 0
 current = start
 for request in service_order:
 total += abs(request - current)
 current = request
 return total

# ============================================
# 1. FCFS (First Come First Served)
# ============================================
def fcfs(requests: List[int], head: int) -> Tuple[List[int], int]:
 """
 Service requests in order of arrival.
 Simple but not optimized.
 """
 service_order = requests.copy() # Process in arrival order
 movement = calculate_head_movement(service_order, head)
 return service_order, movement

# ============================================
# 2. SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First)
# ============================================
def sstf(requests: List[int], head: int) -> Tuple[List[int], int]:
 """
 Always service the request closest to current head position.
 Better than FCFS but may cause starvation.
 """
 remaining = requests.copy()
 service_order = []
 current_head = head
 
 while remaining:
 # Find request closest to current head
 closest = min(remaining, key=lambda x: abs(x - current_head))
 service_order.append(closest)
 current_head = closest
 remaining.remove(closest)
 
 movement = calculate_head_movement(service_order, head)
 return service_order, movement

# ============================================
# 3. SCAN (Elevator Algorithm)
# ============================================
def scan(requests: List[int], head: int, disk_range: int = 199, 
 direction: str = "left") -> Tuple[List[int], int]:
 """
 Head moves in one direction servicing all requests,
 then reverses. Goes to disk edge before reversing.
 """
 remaining = sorted(requests.copy())
 service_order = []
 current_head = head
 
 # Split requests into left and right of head
 left = [r for r in remaining if r < current_head]
 right = [r for r in remaining if r >= current_head]
 
 if direction == "left":
 # Process left first (toward 0), then reverse to right
 # Go to the beginning (0) first
 left_sorted = sorted(left, reverse=True) # Largest first (closest to head)
 
 # Service left side (going toward 0)
 for request in left_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 # Jump to start of disk (0) and then go right
 if left_sorted: # Only if we went left
 current_head = 0 # Jump to disk edge
 
 # Service right side
 for request in right:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 else:
 # Process right first (toward max), then reverse to left
 right_sorted = sorted(right) # Smallest first (closest to head)
 
 for request in right_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 if right_sorted:
 current_head = disk_range # Jump to disk end
 
 for request in sorted(left, reverse=True):
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 movement = calculate_head_movement(service_order, head)
 return service_order, movement

# ============================================
# 4. C-SCAN (Circular SCAN)
# ============================================
def cscan(requests: List[int], head: int, disk_range: int = 199,
 direction: str = "right") -> Tuple[List[int], int]:
 """
 Like SCAN but returns to beginning without servicing on return trip.
 Provides more uniform wait times.
 """
 remaining = sorted(requests.copy())
 service_order = []
 current_head = head
 
 left = [r for r in remaining if r < current_head]
 right = [r for r in remaining if r >= current_head]
 
 if direction == "right":
 # Service all to the right, jump to 0, service remaining left
 right_sorted = sorted(right)
 
 for request in right_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 # Jump to beginning (circular)
 if right_sorted:
 current_head = 0
 
 # Service left side
 left_sorted = sorted(left)
 for request in left_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 else:
 # Mirror image
 left_sorted = sorted(left, reverse=True)
 
 for request in left_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 if left_sorted:
 current_head = disk_range
 
 right_sorted = sorted(right, reverse=True)
 for request in right_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 movement = calculate_head_movement(service_order, head)
 return service_order, movement

# ============================================
# 5. LOOK
# ============================================
def look(requests: List[int], head: int, direction: str = "left") -> Tuple[List[int], int]:
 """
 Like SCAN but doesn't go to disk edge.
 Only goes to the last request in each direction.
 """
 remaining = sorted(requests.copy())
 service_order = []
 current_head = head
 
 left = [r for r in remaining if r < current_head]
 right = [r for r in remaining if r >= current_head]
 
 if direction == "left":
 left_sorted = sorted(left, reverse=True)
 
 # Service left side
 for request in left_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 # Now go right (no jump to disk edge like SCAN)
 for request in right:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 else:
 right_sorted = sorted(right)
 
 for request in right_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 for request in sorted(left, reverse=True):
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 movement = calculate_head_movement(service_order, head)
 return service_order, movement

# ============================================
# 6. C-LOOK
# ============================================
def clook(requests: List[int], head: int, direction: str = "right") -> Tuple[List[int], int]:
 """
 Like C-SCAN but doesn't go to disk edge.
 Jumps from last request to first request on return.
 """
 remaining = sorted(requests.copy())
 service_order = []
 current_head = head
 
 left = [r for r in remaining if r < current_head]
 right = [r for r in remaining if r >= current_head]
 
 if direction == "right":
 right_sorted = sorted(right)
 
 # Service right side
 for request in right_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 # Jump to smallest request (not to 0)
 if right_sorted and left:
 current_head = min(left)
 
 # Service left side
 left_sorted = sorted(left)
 for request in left_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 else:
 left_sorted = sorted(left, reverse=True)
 
 for request in left_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 if left_sorted and right:
 current_head = max(right)
 
 right_sorted = sorted(right, reverse=True)
 for request in right_sorted:
 service_order.append(request)
 current_head = request
 
 movement = calculate_head_movement(service_order, head)
 return service_order, movement

# ============================================
# Main - Compare All Algorithms
# ============================================
def main():
 # Given example data
 requests = [98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67]
 head_start = 53
 disk_range = 199
 
 print("=" * 60)
 print("DISK SCHEDULING ALGORITHMS COMPARISON")
 print("=" * 60)
 print(f"Request Queue: {requests}")
 print(f"Initial Head Position: {head_start}")
 print(f"Disk Range: 0 - {disk_range}")
 print("=" * 60)
 
 results = {}
 
 # Run all algorithms
 results["FCFS"] = fcfs(requests, head_start)
 results["SSTF"] = sstf(requests, head_start)
 results["SCAN (left)"] = scan(requests, head_start, disk_range, "left")
 results["SCAN (right)"] = scan(requests, head_start, disk_range, "right")
 results["C-SCAN (right)"] = cscan(requests, head_start, disk_range, "right")
 results["LOOK (left)"] = look(requests, head_start, "left")
 results["LOOK (right)"] = look(requests, head_start, "right")
 results["C-LOOK (right)"] = clook(requests, head_start, "right")
 
 # Display results
 print(f"\n{'Algorithm':<20} {'Service Order':<35} {'Head Movement':<15}")
 print("-" * 70)
 
 for algo, (order, movement) in results.items():
 print(f"{algo:<20} {str(order):<35} {movement:<15}")
 
 # Find best algorithm
 best = min(results.items(), key=lambda x: x[1][1])
 print(f"\n✓ Best Performance: {best[0]} with {best[1][1]} cylinders movement")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation covers all six major disk scheduling algorithms. FCFS simply processes requests in arrival order. SSTF selects the closest request to minimize seek time. SCAN works like an elevator going to disk edges. C-SCAN is circular - it returns to the start without servicing on return. LOOK is like SCAN but only goes to the last request, not the edge. C-LOOK combines both improvements. The example uses the standard test case from textbooks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) for SSTF, O(n log n) for others

examples.title: C: Implementation of FCFS, SSTF, and SCAN Algorithms
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating three fundamental disk scheduling algorithms with detailed step-by-step output
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates three fundamental disk scheduling algorithms. FCFS processes requests in arrival order with O(n) complexity. SSTF uses a greedy approach selecting the closest request each time, with O(n²) complexity. SCAN (elevator algorithm) moves in one direction servicing all requests, then reverses at the disk boundary. The code includes detailed step-by-step output showing head movement at each step, making it excellent for understanding how each algorithm works.
examples.timeComplexity: FCFS: O(n), SSTF: O(n²), SCAN: O(n log n)

examples.title: Java: Object-Oriented Disk Scheduling with LOOK and C-LOOK
examples.description: Java implementation demonstrating LOOK and C-LOOK algorithms with an object-oriented design and performance metrics comparison
examples.explanation: This Java implementation provides an object-oriented approach to disk scheduling. It implements FCFS, SSTF, LOOK, and C-LOOK algorithms with a clean ScheduleResult class to encapsulate results. LOOK is the most practical algorithm as it doesn't waste time going to disk edges. C-LOOK provides uniform wait times by jumping from the last request to the first request (not to cylinder 0). Modern OS schedulers like Linux's CFQ and deadline schedulers use variants of these algorithms. The implementation includes detailed output showing service order and individual head movements.
examples.timeComplexity: FCFS: O(n), SSTF: O(n²), LOOK/C-LOOK: O(n log n)
